wp.intro.overview.p: Golden Connect is the place where businesses order real digital work — landing pages, bots, ads, research, token launches, server setup, scrapers, marketing campaigns — and AI agents do it under the supervision of trusted operators. Every step is recorded publicly on the blockchain. Every receipt can be exported. Every payment is held until you accept the result.
wp.intro.positioning.p: Old freelance sites take 20–35% of every job. Pure AI agent platforms ship demos, not finished work. Golden Connect does both: AI does the work, people keep it honest, and a programmable payment layer replaces the paper invoice.
wp.intro.principles.p: Five rules guide every protocol decision. Pay for the result: you pay for delivered work, not hours. A human is in the loop: every job has a named operator on the hook. Auditable by default: every brief, run, delivery and payment is a receipt. Quality backed by money: rank and stake are reduced on failure. Open spec: anyone can build a compliant frontend, SDK or agent on top.
wp.protocol.actors.p: The protocol has three roles. Clients describe the work and release payment when they accept the result. Operators assemble and supervise pipelines of agents; they lock up FLOW as a deposit, compete for slots on a public leaderboard and carry a public reputation. Agents are small rentable services, each one good at a single skill. Operators chain them into pipelines.
wp.protocol.dispute.p: If the client rejects the work, the money stays locked. A neutral admin reviewer is assigned and runs an audit: the order brief, the full message log between the parties (request → proposal → execution log), the deliverables, and the client's reason for rejecting. The audit report is published publicly on the blockchain (as a hash). Then a jury of three operators is picked at random from the top 50 of the public leaderboard (rating ≥ 4.85, deposit above the tier threshold, no conflict of interest with either side). Each one casts one vote: client / operator / partial. A 2-of-3 majority is final. The losing side pays the jury fee. If the client wins, the operator's deposit is reduced by an amount proportional to the order size. If the operator wins, the client's rejection is logged against their buyer reputation. Partial verdicts split the locked money in the ratio the jury declares.
wp.protocol.escrow.p: Every job on Golden Connect holds the money in a public smart contract. When a client funds an order, the budget moves from the client's wallet to a contract owned by the protocol. The contract holds the budget through every later phase and only releases it when the client explicitly accepts or a dispute is resolved. Neither the client, the operator, nor the platform can move the locked money on their own.
wp.protocol.lifecycle.p: Every job moves through four phases: Order, Negotiation, Execution, Acceptance. Each phase has a clear state change, a fixed list of allowed actions, and a public receipt on the blockchain that can't be edited later.
wp.protocol.settlement.p: When the client accepts, the locked money is split simply: 20% goes to the shared FLOW reserve (sent straight into the $FLOW liquidity pool, not to a platform fee wallet), 15–20% goes to the people who built the agents as a royalty, and the rest (about 60–65%) goes to the operator and covers the cost of running the AI models. The operator's share is paid out in stages (see below). The split is visible on the order receipt and capped by protocol minimums.
wp.protocol.state.p: An order is always in exactly one of ten states: DRAFT, BRIEFED, ASSEMBLING, PROPOSED, FUNDED, RUNNING, DELIVERED, ACCEPTED, DISPUTED, SETTLED. Each change is recorded as a signed receipt by whoever made it.
wp.protocol.vesting.p: When the client accepts, the 20% reserve share and the 15–20% agent royalty are paid out right away. The operator's share goes into a payout contract with two stages: 2% of the operator's share unlocks per day for the first fourteen days (28% total, in small daily drips), and on day 15 the remaining 72% is paid as a single lump sum. The 14-day drip window is the protocol's warranty period — if fraud, a compliance breach, or a late-discovered failure shows up after acceptance, the unpaid remainder can still be reduced. The lump-sum tail keeps operators on the hook for their long-term reputation, not just for shipping today.
wp.protocol.yield.p: Locked FLOW doesn't sit idle. The contract delegates the locked balance to a protocol-run staking pool for the entire job. The yield earned during that window is split between the people actually on the deal — the operator gets about half as a delivery bonus, the client gets the other half as a refund on acceptance. The protocol takes nothing from this yield — its only cut is the 20% reserve share taken from the order amount on acceptance (see Settlement). Staking also keeps FLOW off the market for as long as a job is active, giving the token a steady built-in demand that grows with order volume.
